content.problemStatement:
Chef embarks on a journey starting from a town named SSS, containing a string of 888 **uppercase** English alphabets. His objective is to reach the destination known as `CODETOWN`.

If Chef is currently in town T1T_1T1​, then, with each move, Chef can transition to another town named T2T_2T2​, provided that **either**:


T2T_2T2​ is derived from T1T_1T1​ by replacing a consonant with another consonant, or;
T2T_2T2​ is derived from T1T_1T1​ by replacing a vowel with another vowel.
Find whether Chef can reach `CODETOWN` in any number of moves.

Note that in the english alphabet, letters `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, and `U` are considered as vowels and rest are considered as consonants.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a string SSS, of length 888 consisting of uppercase english alphabets.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, `YES`, if Chef can reach `CODETOWN`, and `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: S consists of uppercase English alphabets
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a string S of length 8 consisting of uppercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing "YES" if Chef can reach CODETOWN, otherwise "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: YAPETOWN matches the vowel/consonant pattern of CODETOWN, so Chef can transform it step by step. CODECHEF differs at several positions, making transformation impossible. CODETOWN is already the destination.
BADEGILM follows the pattern C V C V C V C C, therefore YES. ABCDEFGH has a vowel at position 1 where CODETOWN expects a consonant, so NO.

solution.approach: The allowed move lets Chef replace a character with another character of the same type (vowel ↔ vowel or consonant ↔ consonant). Therefore each position of the string can be changed independently as long as its vowel/consonant status matches the target. The target string CODETOWN has the pattern: C V C V C V C C (where V denotes a vowel). For a given S we simply check for every index i (0‑based) whether isVowel(S[i]) equals isVowel(TARGET[i]). If all eight positions match, the answer is YES, otherwise NO.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 8) = O(T), because we examine eight characters per test case.
